title: Graves Disease and Urgent Cancer Surgery: Is an Undetectable TSH a Reason to Delay?
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: why normal free T3/T4 (not TSH) defines fitness for GA, why a suppressed TSH alone must not postpone urgent oncologic surgery, the role and limits of raising methimazole and adding a beta-blocker, why Lugol's iodine is for thyroidectomy (not this commando resection), perioperative ATD continuation and a storm plan
authors: Resident/fellowship training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Graves disease on methimazole, scheduled for urgent commando resection with neck dissection.
pmh: Graves disease on methimazole 7.5 mg daily for 1 year
Head-and-neck malignancy requiring composite (commando) resection + neck dissection under general anaesthesia

caseDescription: A 56-year-old woman with Graves disease, on methimazole 7.5 mg daily for a year, is listed for an urgent oncologic commando (composite) resection with neck dissection under general anaesthesia. Her thyroid function shows free T3 and free T4 both normal (in the high-normal range, just below the upper limit), an undetectable TSH, a resting pulse of 86/min and no cardiac symptoms. The resident is weighing three options: (1) increase methimazole to 10 mg, add propranolol LA 40 mg, declare her fit and proceed now with close anaesthetic monitoring; (2) defer surgery and start that regimen first; or simply (3) proceed. The teaching question is whether the undetectable TSH should delay an urgent cancer operation.
keyFindings: Free T3 and free T4 both normal on methimazole = biochemically controlled (euthyroid) Graves disease — fit in principle for GA
Undetectable TSH in isolation does NOT define thyrotoxicosis and is not a reason to defer urgent surgery — TSH lags for months after control is restored
Resting pulse 86, no cardiac disease, no thyrotoxic symptoms — low perioperative thyroid-storm risk
Raising methimazole 7.5→10 mg gives no acute protection (antithyroid drugs block synthesis, not release; effect over days–weeks) — she is already euthyroid
A beta-blocker (e.g. propranolol) is reasonable to blunt perioperative sympathetic surges if no contraindication; pulse 86 is acceptable
This is a commando resection, NOT a thyroidectomy — preoperative Lugol's iodine (to reduce thyroid-gland vascularity) is not indicated here
finalDiagnosis: Biochemically euthyroid Graves disease (normal free T3/T4 on methimazole) with an isolated undetectable TSH, presenting for urgent oncologic surgery. The correct decision is to proceed with the cancer operation after standard optimisation — continue (optionally modestly increase) methimazole, consider a perioperative beta-blocker if tolerated, check a recent CBC/differential and LFTs, and ensure close anaesthetic monitoring with a thyroid-storm plan available. The undetectable TSH must not by itself postpone surgery, because fitness is defined by normal free hormones and clinical status, not by a TSH that physiologically lags. Surgery should be deferred only if free hormones are elevated, the patient is clinically thyrotoxic/tachycardic, there is uncontrolled cardiac disease or storm, or there is antithyroid-drug toxicity (agranulocytosis/hepatotoxicity).
learningObjectives: Define perioperative thyroid fitness by free T3/T4 and clinical status, not by the TSH.
Explain why an undetectable TSH lags and must not delay urgent oncologic surgery in an otherwise euthyroid patient.
State the role and the acute limits of increasing methimazole and adding a beta-blocker before surgery.
Recognise that Lugol's iodine/potassium iodide is a thyroidectomy-specific preparation, not required for non-thyroid (commando) surgery.
Plan perioperative antithyroid-drug continuation across the NPO period and keep a thyroid-storm protocol and clear deferral criteria ready.

items.title: Why the free T3/T4 — not the TSH — tell you she is fit for anaesthesia
items.content: The whole decision turns on reading the right number. Free T3 and free T4 are the real-time measures of circulating thyroid hormone and of metabolic/cardiovascular thyrotoxic risk; here they are both normal, so she is biochemically euthyroid and, in thyroid terms, fit for general anaesthesia. The undetectable TSH is not a second, contradictory result that overrides this — it is a lagging pituitary signal. After a period of thyrotoxicosis the thyrotrophs stay suppressed for weeks to months even once free hormones have normalised on treatment, so a suppressed TSH alongside normal free hormones simply reflects recent (now-controlled) Graves disease, not current dangerous thyrotoxicosis. Anchoring the fitness decision on the free hormones, with the TSH understood as a slow trailing indicator, is the core teaching point.
keyPoints: Free T3/T4 = current thyroid status and perioperative risk — both normal here
TSH lags weeks–months after control; suppression ≠ current thyrotoxicosis
Judge fitness on free hormones + clinical state, not TSH

items.title: Why an undetectable TSH must not delay urgent cancer surgery
items.content: Deferring an urgent oncologic operation to 'wait for the TSH to recover' would be a serious error, because that TSH may stay suppressed for months and the delay directly harms the cancer outcome (tumour progression, missed resection window). The defensible reasons to postpone surgery are thyroid-functional and clinical — elevated free hormones, clinical thyrotoxicosis, resting tachycardia >100–110, atrial fibrillation/heart failure/ischaemia, overt thyroid storm, or antithyroid-drug toxicity — none of which she has. An isolated biochemical abnormality (suppressed TSH with normal free hormones, no symptoms, pulse 86) is not among them. The principle generalises: do not let a lagging or isolated lab value postpone time-critical surgery when the patient is clinically and functionally controlled.
keyPoints: TSH can stay suppressed for months — waiting for it harms cancer outcomes
Valid deferral reasons are functional/clinical, not an isolated suppressed TSH
She meets none of the deferral criteria — proceed

items.title: Why raising methimazole to 10 mg gives ongoing — not acute — protection
items.content: Increasing methimazole from 7.5 to 10 mg is a reasonable, low-risk tweak if you want extra margin given high-normal free hormones, but residents must understand what it does and does not do. Methimazole inhibits new hormone synthesis (thyroid peroxidase); it does not block release of hormone already stored in the gland, so its effect builds over days to weeks and provides no acute, same-day protection. In a patient who is already euthyroid on 7.5 mg, the increase is therefore optional ongoing optimisation, not an urgent pre-operative manoeuvre — and it should not become a reason to delay surgery while 'waiting for it to work'. Continuing her established methimazole through the perioperative period is the essential part; the dose bump is secondary.
keyPoints: Methimazole blocks synthesis, not release — effect over days–weeks, not acute
She is already euthyroid; 7.5→10 mg is optional ongoing optimisation
Don't delay surgery to 'wait for' the higher dose to act

items.title: Why (and when) to add propranolol before surgery
items.content: A beta-blocker such as propranolol is the agent that gives rapid perioperative protection, because it blunts the adrenergic features of thyroid hormone excess (tachycardia, tremor, hypertension) and, at higher doses, modestly reduces peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion. Adding propranolol LA 40 mg is reasonable here to cover sympathetic surges during laryngoscopy, resection and extubation, provided there is no contraindication — asthma/COPD (non-selective beta-blockade), significant bradycardia, high-grade heart block, or decompensated heart failure. Her resting pulse of 86 with no cardiac disease is acceptable and tolerant of a beta-blocker; a common target is a heart rate under ~90/min. If oral intake is interrupted, the beta-blocker can be continued by another route or covered intra-operatively with an intravenous agent (e.g. esmolol/metoprolol) per anaesthesia.
keyPoints: Beta-blocker = the rapidly acting perioperative protection (controls adrenergic surge)
Reasonable if no asthma/COPD, bradycardia, heart block or decompensated HF; pulse 86 fine
If NPO, continue by alternative route or use IV esmolol/metoprolol intra-op

items.title: Why Lugol's iodine is not needed here — this is not a thyroidectomy
items.content: A frequent reflex is to 'prepare the thyroid patient' with Lugol's iodine or potassium iodide before surgery. That preparation is specific to thyroidectomy: iodine (via the Wolff–Chaikoff effect) acutely reduces hormone release and, importantly, decreases the vascularity and friability of the thyroid gland to make the gland itself safer to operate on. This patient is having a commando (composite oral-cavity/head-and-neck) resection with neck dissection — the thyroid gland is not the operative target — so the vascularity rationale does not apply and routine preoperative iodine is not indicated. Misapplying thyroidectomy prep to non-thyroid surgery is the trap; iodine is reserved for genuine thyroid-gland surgery or for rapid control in thyroid storm, not for routine GA in a controlled Graves patient.
keyPoints: Lugol's/KI reduces thyroid gland vascularity — a thyroidectomy-specific benefit
Commando resection does not operate on the thyroid, so iodine prep is not indicated
Reserve iodine for thyroid-gland surgery or thyroid storm

items.title: How to keep the antithyroid drug going across the NPO period
items.content: The one thing that must not lapse is thyroid control, so plan continuity of methimazole around the fasting and post-operative period. Give the morning dose if oral intake is permitted preoperatively; if the patient is NPO post-operatively, deliver methimazole by an enteral route through the nasogastric/feeding tube (it is well absorbed) once the surgical team allows, and resume oral dosing as soon as swallowing is safe — relevant after a commando resection where oral intake is delayed and an NG/feeding route is usually already in place. Methimazole can also be compounded for rectal administration, and parenteral options exist in specialist settings if enteral access is unavailable. The beta-blocker is bridged intravenously as needed. Brief interruptions are tolerated because stored hormone buffers short gaps, but a clear written plan prevents an unintended multi-day cessation.
keyPoints: Plan methimazole continuity through fasting/NPO — don't let control lapse
Use the NG/feeding tube (well absorbed); rectal/parenteral routes exist if needed
Bridge the beta-blocker IV; a written plan prevents accidental multi-day stoppage

items.title: Why her thyroid-storm risk is low — and how to keep a plan ready anyway
items.content: Thyroid storm is the feared perioperative complication, precipitated by the stress of surgery/anaesthesia in an inadequately controlled hyperthyroid patient. The key reassurance here is that storm arises from uncontrolled thyrotoxicosis, and she is euthyroid (normal free hormones, pulse 86, no symptoms) — so her risk is low. That does not mean ignoring it: the anaesthetic plan should still anticipate and treat sympathetic surges and hyperthermia, monitor continuously (ECG, blood pressure, temperature), and have a thyroid-storm protocol immediately available. The Burch–Wartofsky score helps recognise evolving storm (fever, tachyarrhythmia, CNS and GI/hepatic features). The teaching balance is proportionate: do not over-prepare a controlled patient as if she were thyrotoxic, but keep the rescue pathway ready because the operative stress is significant.
keyPoints: Storm needs uncontrolled thyrotoxicosis as substrate — she is euthyroid, so risk is low
Still monitor (ECG/BP/temp), treat surges/hyperthermia, keep a storm protocol ready
Burch–Wartofsky score aids recognition of evolving storm

items.title: How to complete the workup: the methimazole safety labs and cardiac check
items.content: Two practical pre-operative additions complete the picture. First, methimazole-specific safety bloods if not recent: a CBC with differential (agranulocytosis is the dangerous idiosyncratic effect — relevant before major surgery with infection risk) and liver function tests (hepatotoxicity), so you are not taking an unrecognised cytopenia or hepatitis into theatre. Second, a baseline cardiac assessment proportionate to major head-and-neck surgery: ECG (looking for atrial fibrillation or ischaemia), vital-sign and symptom review, with arterial-line/ICU planning per the magnitude of the resection. Document the endocrine clearance explicitly — euthyroid on methimazole, free hormones normal, fit to proceed — so the suppressed TSH does not trigger a last-minute, inappropriate cancellation by another team.
keyPoints: Check CBC/differential and LFTs (methimazole agranulocytosis/hepatotoxicity) if not recent
Baseline ECG/vitals; escalate monitoring (arterial line/ICU) per surgical magnitude
Document explicit endocrine clearance so the suppressed TSH isn't misread later

items.title: Why 'proceed after optimisation' beats both 'defer' and 'proceed unprepared'
items.content: Lay the three options side by side. Deferring the cancer surgery to chase TSH recovery is wrong — it harms the oncologic outcome for no thyroid-safety gain in an already-euthyroid patient. Proceeding with no preparation underuses cheap, effective risk reduction (a beta-blocker for surgical sympathetic surges, continuity of methimazole, baseline safety labs, an anaesthetic storm plan). The middle path — proceed now, after standard optimisation — captures the safety benefit without the harmful delay and is the correct answer. Framed as a principle: in a controlled thyroid patient facing urgent surgery, optimise rather than postpone, and let the operative urgency, not a lagging lab, set the timeline.
keyPoints: Defer = harms cancer outcome for no thyroid gain; proceed-unprepared = misses easy safety
Proceed after optimisation (beta-blocker, ATD continuity, labs, storm plan) is correct
Optimise, don't postpone, when the patient is already controlled

items.question: Why is an undetectable TSH, alone, not a valid reason to postpone this urgent operation?
items.answer: Because TSH is a lagging indicator and, in isolation, does not measure current thyrotoxic risk. After a period of thyrotoxicosis the pituitary thyrotrophs remain suppressed for weeks to months even once free T3 and free T4 have normalised on antithyroid therapy, so an undetectable TSH alongside normal free hormones simply reflects recent, now-controlled Graves disease — not dangerous current thyrotoxicosis. Perioperative risk (tachyarrhythmia, storm) tracks the free hormones and clinical state, both of which are reassuring here (normal free T3/T4, pulse 86, no symptoms, no cardiac disease). Waiting for the TSH to recover could take months and would harm the cancer outcome, so the suppressed TSH must not gate the surgery. Surgery is deferred only for functional/clinical reasons — elevated free hormones, clinical thyrotoxicosis, resting HR >100–110, AF/heart failure/ischaemia, storm, or antithyroid-drug toxicity — none of which she has.
keyPoints: TSH lags weeks–months after free hormones normalise — suppression ≠ current risk
Risk tracks free T3/T4 + clinical state, both reassuring here
Defer only for functional/clinical reasons, not an isolated suppressed TSH

items.question: How would you optimise and clear this patient for urgent commando surgery?
items.answer: Proceed after standard optimisation rather than deferring. (1) Confirm she is euthyroid on free T3/T4 (she is) and document explicit endocrine clearance so the suppressed TSH is not misread as a contraindication. (2) Continue methimazole through the perioperative period — give it preoperatively if intake is allowed and plan an enteral (NG/feeding-tube) route post-operatively; a modest increase to 10 mg is optional ongoing optimisation, not an acute fix. (3) Add a beta-blocker (e.g. propranolol) if no contraindication (asthma/COPD, bradycardia, heart block, decompensated heart failure) to blunt perioperative sympathetic surges; pulse 86 is acceptable, target HR <~90, and bridge with IV esmolol/metoprolol if NPO. (4) Check a recent CBC/differential and LFTs for methimazole safety, and a baseline ECG. (5) Do NOT give Lugol's iodine — that is thyroidectomy preparation, not relevant to this non-thyroid resection. (6) Arrange close anaesthetic monitoring (ECG, BP, temperature) with a thyroid-storm protocol available, though storm risk is low in a euthyroid patient.
keyPoints: Document euthyroid clearance; continue methimazole (enteral route if NPO)
Add beta-blocker if tolerated; check CBC/LFT and ECG; no Lugol's iodine
Close anaesthetic monitoring + storm plan; proceed, don't defer

items.stem: A 56-year-old woman with Graves disease on methimazole 7.5 mg has normal free T3 and free T4, an undetectable TSH, pulse 86 and no cardiac disease, and needs urgent commando resection with neck dissection. What is the best course?
options: Proceed with surgery after standard optimisation; the undetectable TSH alone is not a reason to delay
Defer surgery until the TSH normalises
Cancel surgery and refer back to endocrinology for months of further treatment
Proceed only after radioactive iodine ablation of the thyroid
items.explanation: She is biochemically euthyroid (normal free hormones) and clinically stable, so she is fit for urgent oncologic surgery after standard optimisation. TSH lags for months after control is restored, so an isolated suppressed TSH is not a reason to delay; deferring or ablating the thyroid would harm the cancer outcome.

items.stem: Which best explains the undetectable TSH despite normal free T3/T4 in this patient?
options: Pituitary thyrotrophs remain suppressed for weeks–months after thyrotoxicosis is controlled (TSH lags)
She is currently in overt thyrotoxicosis requiring emergency treatment
She has developed central hypothyroidism
The methimazole has caused TSH-secreting adenoma
items.explanation: After thyrotoxicosis, TSH stays suppressed for weeks to months even once free hormones normalise on therapy, so a suppressed TSH with normal free T3/T4 reflects recent, now-controlled Graves disease. It does not indicate current overt thyrotoxicosis or central hypothyroidism.

items.stem: What is the correct understanding of increasing methimazole from 7.5 to 10 mg before this urgent surgery?
options: It is optional ongoing optimisation and gives no acute protection, because it blocks synthesis, not release
It rapidly renders her safe for same-day surgery
It acutely blocks release of stored thyroid hormone
It must be done before surgery can ever proceed
items.explanation: Methimazole inhibits new hormone synthesis, not release of stored hormone, so its effect builds over days to weeks and provides no same-day protection. In an already-euthyroid patient the increase is optional ongoing optimisation and must not be used to justify delaying surgery.

items.stem: Why is adding propranolol a reasonable perioperative measure here?
options: It rapidly blunts adrenergic surges (tachycardia, tremor, hypertension) during surgery and is tolerated given pulse 86 and no cardiac disease
It permanently cures the underlying Graves disease
It is mandatory in every euthyroid patient before any surgery
It replaces the need to continue methimazole
items.explanation: A beta-blocker gives rapid control of the adrenergic features of thyroid hormone excess and covers perioperative sympathetic surges; with pulse 86 and no contraindication it is well tolerated. It does not cure Graves disease, is not mandatory for every patient, and does not replace antithyroid-drug continuity.

items.stem: Should this patient receive preoperative Lugol's iodine?
options: No — iodine reduces thyroid-gland vascularity for thyroidectomy and is not indicated for this non-thyroid commando resection
Yes — all Graves patients need iodine before any operation
Yes — to permanently lower her thyroid hormone levels
Yes — because her TSH is undetectable
items.explanation: Lugol's iodine/potassium iodide is given before thyroidectomy to reduce gland vascularity and friability (and acutely lower hormone release). The thyroid is not the operative target in a commando resection, so routine iodine prep is not indicated; it is reserved for thyroid-gland surgery or thyroid storm.

items.stem: Which finding WOULD justify deferring this urgent surgery on thyroid grounds?
options: Elevated free T3/T4 with resting tachycardia >110 and clinical thyrotoxicosis
An undetectable TSH with normal free hormones
A resting pulse of 86 with no symptoms
A one-year history of methimazole therapy
items.explanation: Deferral is justified by functional/clinical instability — elevated free hormones, clinical thyrotoxicosis, resting HR >100–110, AF/heart failure/ischaemia, storm, or antithyroid-drug toxicity. An isolated suppressed TSH with normal free hormones, a pulse of 86, or simply being on long-term methimazole are not reasons to delay.

items.statement: Normal free T3 and free T4 with an undetectable TSH indicate the patient is biochemically euthyroid and, in thyroid terms, fit for general anaesthesia.
items.explanation: True. Free T3/T4 reflect current thyroid status and perioperative risk; when both are normal the patient is euthyroid and fit for GA. The suppressed TSH is a lagging indicator and does not change that, provided the patient is clinically stable.

items.statement: Increasing methimazole the day before surgery rapidly protects the patient against intraoperative thyrotoxicosis.
items.explanation: False. Methimazole blocks hormone synthesis, not release of stored hormone, so its effect develops over days to weeks. Rapid perioperative protection comes from a beta-blocker, not from acutely raising the antithyroid-drug dose.

items.assertion: This patient's urgent cancer surgery should not be postponed solely because her TSH is undetectable.
items.reason: TSH can remain suppressed for months after thyrotoxicosis is controlled, so it does not reflect her current, normal thyroid hormone levels.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because TSH lags for months after free hormones normalise, an isolated suppressed TSH does not indicate current thyrotoxic risk; with normal free T3/T4 and clinical stability she is fit, and delaying urgent oncologic surgery for a lagging lab would only harm the cancer outcome. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: Preoperative Lugol's iodine should be given to this patient before her commando resection.
items.reason: Iodine is required before all operations in Graves disease to prevent thyroid storm.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Lugol's iodine is a thyroidectomy-specific preparation (reducing gland vascularity and acutely lowering hormone release), not a universal pre-operative requirement in Graves disease, and it is not indicated for a non-thyroid commando resection. Routine perioperative control comes from antithyroid-drug continuity and a beta-blocker, not iodine. Hence option 4.

items.question: Summarise the recommended decision and the reasoning in this case.
items.answer: Proceed with the urgent commando surgery after standard optimisation; do not defer for the undetectable TSH. She is biochemically euthyroid (normal free T3/T4) and clinically stable (pulse 86, no cardiac disease, no thyrotoxic symptoms), so she is fit for GA. The suppressed TSH is a lagging indicator that can stay low for months after control and does not reflect current risk, so waiting for it would only harm the cancer outcome. Optimisation means continuing methimazole (enteral route if NPO; a modest increase is optional), adding a beta-blocker if tolerated to cover sympathetic surges, checking CBC/LFTs and an ECG, and ensuring close anaesthetic monitoring with a storm plan — but not Lugol's iodine, which is for thyroidectomy.

items.question: List the findings that WOULD justify deferring surgery on thyroid grounds.
items.answer: Elevated free T3/free T4 (overt biochemical thyrotoxicosis); clinical thyrotoxicosis or thyroid storm (e.g. high Burch–Wartofsky score); resting tachycardia >100–110/min; atrial fibrillation, heart failure or myocardial ischaemia; severe uncontrolled adrenergic symptoms; or antithyroid-drug toxicity such as agranulocytosis or hepatotoxicity. An isolated undetectable TSH with normal free hormones and a stable patient is not on this list.

items.question: How should methimazole and beta-blockade be managed across the NPO perioperative period?
items.answer: Maintain continuity of thyroid control. Give methimazole preoperatively if oral intake is allowed; if the patient is NPO post-operatively, deliver it enterally via the nasogastric/feeding tube (well absorbed) once permitted — readily arranged after a commando resection where a feeding route is usually present — and resume oral dosing when swallowing is safe; rectal/parenteral routes exist if enteral access is unavailable. Bridge the beta-blocker intravenously (e.g. esmolol or metoprolol) during the period of no oral intake to keep heart rate controlled. A clear written plan prevents an unintended multi-day cessation of the antithyroid drug.

items.stem: Why is an undetectable TSH, alone, not a valid reason to postpone urgent surgery in a patient with controlled Graves disease?
options: A) Because TSH is a lagging indicator and does not reflect current thyrotoxic risk
B) Because TSH suppression is a direct marker of ongoing thyrotoxicosis
C) Because waiting for TSH normalization is required before surgery to avoid storm
D) Because TSH levels correlate directly with perioperative cardiac risk
items.explanation: TSH is suppressed for weeks to months after free T3/T4 normalize, reflecting prior thyrotoxicosis rather than current risk. Perioperative risk depends on current free hormone levels and clinical status, not TSH alone.

items.stem: Which of the following is the correct approach to optimise and clear a euthyroid patient with controlled hyperthyroidism for urgent non-thyroid surgery?
options: A) Continue methimazole (enteral route if NPO), check CBC/LFT and ECG, add beta-blocker if tolerated, avoid Lugol's iodine, and proceed without deferring
B) Discontinue methimazole preoperatively, administer Lugol's iodine, and defer surgery until thyroid function normalises
C) Use beta-blocker alone, avoid methimazole, and proceed without endocrine clearance documentation
D) Administer Lugol's iodine, check ECG only if arrhythmia is present, and proceed without methimazole
items.explanation: Correct management includes continuing methimazole (enteral route if NPO), checking CBC/LFT and ECG, adding beta-blocker if tolerated, avoiding Lugol's iodine (which is for thyroidectomy), and proceeding without deferral in a euthyroid patient. Discontinuing methimazole, using Lugol's iodine, or omitting endocrine clearance documentation are incorrect.

items.stem: A 45-year-old woman with papillary thyroid carcinoma and suppressed TSH (undetectable) is scheduled for urgent commando surgery. Her free T3/T4 are normal, pulse is 86, and she has no cardiac disease or thyrotoxic symptoms. What is the most appropriate management strategy?
options: A) Proceed with surgery after standard optimisation (methimazole, beta-blocker if tolerated, CBC/LFTs, ECG) without deferring for TSH
B) Defer surgery until TSH normalises with thyroid hormone replacement
C) Administer Lugol's iodine preoperatively to prevent thyroid storm
D) Cancel surgery and initiate definitive antithyroid therapy with propylthiouracil
items.explanation: The suppressed TSH is a lagging indicator that does not reflect current thyroid function or cancer risk. The patient is biochemically euthyroid and clinically stable, making her fit for general anaesthesia. Lugol's iodine is contraindicated in this context, and thyroid hormone replacement would worsen cancer outcomes.

items.stem: Which of the following findings would justify deferring thyroid surgery?
options: A) Elevated free T3/free T4 (overt biochemical thyrotoxicosis) or clinical thyrotoxicosis
B) Isolated undetectable TSH with normal free T3/T4 and stable patient
C) Mild diffuse goiter without functional abnormalities
D) History of previous thyroidectomy for benign nodule
items.explanation: Overt thyrotoxicosis, thyroid storm, cardiac complications, and drug toxicity are indications for deferring surgery. An isolated undetectable TSH with normal hormones and stable patient does not require deferral.

Okay, let's tackle this query. The user wants me to convert the given Q&A into an objective exam question in JSON format. The original question is about findings that would justify deferring thyroid surgery. items.stem: What is the appropriate management of methimazole and beta-blockade during the NPO perioperative period?
options: A) Continue methimazole via enteral administration if oral intake is restricted, use IV beta-blockers during NPO periods, and maintain a written plan to avoid drug cessation
B) Discontinue methimazole during NPO periods and resume oral beta-blockade once oral intake is permitted
C) Administer methimazole parenterally and use oral beta-blockers during NPO periods
D) Use rectal methimazole and discontinue beta-blockade if heart rate remains stable
items.explanation: Methimazole should be continued via enteral routes during NPO periods to maintain thyroid control, while IV beta-blockers (e.g., esmolol/metoprolol) are required during fasting to manage heart rate. Discontinuation or incorrect routes increase risk of thyroid storm or arrhythmias.
